hypothesis: Cross-sectional momentum (buying recent winners, selling recent losers) generates persistent alpha. Scaling position sizes by inverse volatility improves risk-adjusted returns by equalizing risk contribution across positions.
universe: S&P 500 constituents or broad ETF basket
entry_logic: Rank by 12-month return, skipping the most recent month (to avoid short-term reversal). Long top quintile, short bottom quintile.
exit_logic: Monthly rebalance. Positions exit when they leave the top/bottom quintile.
position_sizing: Inverse volatility weighting within each leg. Target total portfolio volatility of 10% annualized.
risk_management: Crash protection: reduce exposure by 50% if the broad market's 1-month return is below -10%. Max single position 5%.
known_failure_modes: Momentum crashes: sharp reversals (like March 2009) cause catastrophic losses in momentum portfolios.
Crowded trade: momentum is the most well-known anomaly, leading to reduced alpha over time.
High turnover: monthly rebalance of quintile portfolios generates significant transaction costs.
Short-term reversal effect: without skipping the most recent month, performance degrades.

historical_performance.notes: The skip-recent-month tweak is critical. Without it, Sharpe drops to ~0.8. Volatility scaling adds ~0.3 to Sharpe. Crash protection helps in 2008-2009 but may cause whipsaws.

references: Stefan Jansen - ML for Algorithmic Trading, Chapter 4
Jegadeesh & Titman (1993) - Returns to Buying Winners and Selling Losers
